message.content: Good morning Fable!  I am looking forward to mulling an approach to bedrock with you.  Would you first locate the memo about this?  Wow I touched it yesterday but don't remember its title.  Maybe review RBS0*.adoc too, that is a context for it.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# Bedrock Quire — Consume-Side Shaping
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-09
4	
5	Status: intent/shaping. Nothing here is built. This memo records the *shape* of a
6	future Recipe Bottle subsystem for safely consuming frontier models on AWS Bedrock,
7	so the durable conclusions survive into the roadmap and a later build. It supersedes
8	nothing; it **extends** `memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (which predates
9	the identity model and the consume/host split) and **rides** the citizen/capability
10	identity model of heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`).
11	
12	This is a *successor* to the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, not an edit of it — that memo is a
13	dated snapshot and keeps its provenance. Where the two disagree, this one is current.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 1. Scope — consume, not host
18	
19	The need is **safe, private use of frontier models** (Claude and peers on Bedrock) for the
20	operator and the operator's users. Conversations must stay private. That is the whole point.
21	
22	Explicitly **in scope**: invoking *native foundation models already on Bedrock* from a
23	contained client.
24	
25	Explicitly **out of scope** (collapses away because we are not hosting our own weights):
26	the embassy / bullion / entrust / Custom-Model-Import delivery path. We are not pushing
27	models to AWS. The roadmap's `chantry` entry imagined both a custom-imported model and a
28	native one; only the native-consume half lives here.
29	
30	A consequence worth stating plainly up front (it corrects the operator's initial mental
31	model): **Bedrock is not an "instance" you run and keep secure.** It is a fully managed,
32	serverless, *stateless* request/response API. There is no box to harden, and — per Recipe
33	Bottle's **asymmetric-sovereignty** stance — no AWS principal Recipe Bottle owns. What you
34	control is: which model you invoke, the IAM perimeter, the network path, the logging switch,
35	and the AWS account itself. "Secure within Recipe Bottle" therefore splits: the *consuming*
36	end is secured inside a bottle; the *serving* end is yours in AWS, stood up from
37	Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates but owned by you wearing an AWS hat.
38	
39	## 2. Why Recipe Bottle holds the persistent state
40	
41	Bedrock is stateless: each call is independent, and even the conversation history is held by
42	the *client* and re-sent every turn. So the durable truth of "which model, reached how, under
43	whose identity, at what cost ceiling" has **nowhere to live on the Bedrock side.** That is the
44	argument for a persistent Recipe Bottle config model: statelessness over there forces
45	persistence over here.
46	
47	## 3. The privacy model — six boundaries, one Palisade
48	
49	"Completely private" decomposes into distinct boundaries with distinct strengths. The first
50	four can be driven to near-airtight; the last two are the honest ceiling.
51	
52	- **A — From the public internet (network).** PrivateLink keeps traffic on the AWS network;
53	  allowlist + TLS is the public-path fallback. Strong; delivered client-side by the conduit.
54	- **B — From the model provider.** Architectural: Bedrock runs each provider's model in an
55	  isolated deployment account the provider cannot reach; zero provider operator access. Under
56	  Commercial Terms (which Bedrock is), the provider does not train on your data. Strong.
57	- **C — From your own logs.** Bedrock defaults to zero-data-retention; model-invocation
58	  logging is opt-in, off by default. The main self-inflicted leak is turning logging on and
59	  under-securing the sink. Default: leave it off.
60	- **D — From AWS retention/training/sharing.** Contractual + ZDR; abuse detection is fully
61	  automated, no human review, no storage (verify per model — a few carry a 30-day
62	  classifier-flagged exception; Claude is in the clean automated-only camp).
63	- **E — From AWS hardware at the moment of inference.** The boundary **no hosted frontier
64	  model can cross.** The model must read your plaintext to answer; AWS silicon processes
65	  cleartext. The guarantee is *contractual + architectural + compliance-audited* — genuinely
66	  strong, the same class of trust under all enterprise cloud — but trust *placed*, not trust
67	  *eliminated*. If the threat model requires "the operator mathematically cannot see it,"
68	  nothing hosted meets that.
69	- **F — From server-side agency egress (NEW).** Plain inference (`Converse`/`InvokeModel`)
70	  has *zero* network reach — the model is a pure prompt→completion function and even a tool
71	  *decision* never touches the wire. But agentic surfaces — Bedrock Agents with Lambda action
72	  groups, and AgentCore Browser / Code Interpreter / the server-side tool execution of the
73	  Responses API — execute **server-side** and can reach the open internet **behind the
74	  Palisade, entirely outside the sentry's view.** This is opt-in (a different, more complex
75	  API surface; you never get it by accident), and the **only** lever over it is **IAM**, not
76	  the sentry.
77	
78	**The Palisade.** Boundaries A, C, and the client side of B/D sit inside our realm and get
79	full sovereignty. E is irreducible foreign ground — characterize and contain, do not pretend
80	to govern. F is the subtle one: the sentry governs the *bottle's* egress and is structurally
81	blind to *Bedrock's* server-side agency, so F is governed only by IAM and by *not invoking
82	agentic surfaces*. The private Quire uses non-agentic surfaces only; agentic Bedrock is a
83	deliberate, named re-opening of the egress question on AWS's side of the Pale.
84	
85	## 4. What Recipe Bottle does — and does not — do
86	
87	Recipe Bottle **establishes and maintains the binding**: the config, the identity, the reach,
88	the cost envelope. The **inference calls are made by whatever runs inside the bottle** (an
89	app, a chat client, an orchestrator) — *not* by Recipe Bottle. There is therefore **no
90	"submit" verb**. Recipe Bottle's surface ends at "the path and identity are ready and
91	verified." The only place Recipe Bottle itself touches Bedrock is a one-shot *probe* (does it
92	answer?), a health check, not a workload call.
93	
94	This single rule shapes the whole vocabulary: the verbs are *lifecycle of the binding*, never
95	invocation.
96	
97	## 5. Vocabulary
98	
99	The roadmap's working words `chantry` (target) and `conduit` (reach) both start with `C`,
100	which stumbles against Crucible. Renamed to unbound primary letters:
101	
102	| Concept | Noun | Verbs | Register | Cardinality |
103	|---|---|---|---|---|
104	| The model endpoint a bottle consumes | **Quire** | `endow` / `dissolve` | ecclesiastical (heir to *chantry*'s "where the voice issues") | 0..N |
105	| The peer-bearing private reach into an AWS VPC | **Outpost** | `raise` / `strike` | camp/diplomatic (sibling to *embassy*) | 0..1 |
106	
107	All four verbs are collision-free across RB and JJK today (`grep` lands clean — the
108	findability rationale the operator asked us to favor).
109	
110	**Two tiers of lifecycle.** The Outpost is the heavy, shared, singleton reach (raise once,
111	strike once). Quires are the light bindings that ride it (endow/dissolve freely). This is the
112	conduit-vs-target split, resolved: the reach object is first-class, not footprint — its 0..1
113	cardinality is exactly what earns it a noun and its own verbs.
114	
115	**The peer is *not* separately named.** It is the Outpost's compute; `raise`/`strike` operate
116	on the Outpost, which includes the peer. No peer-lifecycle verbs — the peer is hourly-cheap,
117	stop/start saves only noise, and the endpoint/EIP are standing anyway.
118	
119	**Prefix allocation (open minting item).** A Quire regime is a natural sibling to
120	nameplate/vessel; `RBRQ` is a free slot in the `RBR*` family. The Outpost cannot be `RBRO`
121	(taken by the OAuth regime), so its config home needs a non-`RBRO` slot — deferred to the
122	later minting pass.
123	
124	## 6. Config shape — three orthogonal layers
125	
126	A "Bedrock configuration" is not one blob; it is three independently-variable layers (same
127	model reachable two ways; same reach under two identities; same identity to three models).
128	They want composable homes, not a monolith.
129	
130	- **Target — the Quire.** Fields: model(s); version-pin (Bedrock model IDs drift — pin for
131	  provenance, as we pin everything else); region + inference profile (single-region or
132	  geographic, **not global** by default — a silent residency/privacy knob); **`agency`** (see
133	  below); a capability reference (who may invoke); a reach selector; logging posture
134	  (`off` by default).
135	- **Reach — `allowlist` or `outpost`.** This extends the nameplate's network posture. The
136	  MVP value is `allowlist` with **no Outpost in existence**; the fast-follow raises an Outpost
137	  and repoints. Egress allowlist scope for a private Quire: the Bedrock endpoint, plus the STS
138	  endpoint if federating, and **nothing else** — the egress-lockdown crown jewel.
139	- **Identity — the invoking principal.** MVP: a confined credential placed in the bottle
140	  (RBRA-style regime), because ₣BZ's federation is future. Fast-follow: federated short-lived
141	  STS (the envoy pattern), no resting key. Either way the identity is decoupled from
142	  capability per ₣BZ.
143	
144	**The `agency` field (boundary F's declared home).** A lattice, not a boolean:
145	
146	- `none` — plain inference, no server-side tools. (privacy-max; the MVP default and the only
147	  MVP value)
148	- `internal` — server-side tools, but only your resources (e.g. a knowledge base over your
149	  data); no internet.
150	- `internet` — server-side tools that reach the open web (AgentCore Browser, etc.).
151	  (privacy-off)
152	
153	`agency` is *declared intent that IAM enforces* — the same intent-vs-enforcement spine as
154	₣BZ. The Quire's invocation principal carries an agency clause; an audit can diff declared vs.
155	actual IAM. Even though every MVP Quire is `agency: none`, declaring it earns its keep: it
156	makes the privacy guarantee an explicit, enforced property rather than an absence, and gives
157	the IAM *denial* a documented home.
158	
159	**Note the two outward directions, kept distinct.** `reach` is how the *bottle* gets *in* to
160	Bedrock (sentry-governed). `agency` is how far the *model* may act *out* (IAM-governed). Same
161	Quire, opposite directions, one field each — do not let one blur into the other.
162	
163	## 7. Staging
164	
165	**MVP** — allowlist Quire, single Quire, `agency: none`, plain inference, confined credential
166	in the bottle, no Outpost, no VPC, no standing AWS infrastructure. This already delivers the
167	privacy story: boundaries A (TLS + egress-lockdown), B, C, D hold; the bottle excludes the
168	workstation from the data path and can leak nowhere but Bedrock. **The VPC is not what keeps
169	conversations private — this MVP already does.**
170	
171	**Fast-follow** — raise an Outpost (PrivateLink), repoint Quires to `reach: outpost`, and
172	upgrade identity to federation. Build the VPC only when one of three triggers fires:
173	1. egress **exclusivity** is required — allowlisting Bedrock by static CIDR is ≈ "all of
174	   Amazon" (no dedicated published range); domain/SNI is tighter but shares IPs; PrivateLink
175	   is the one *exclusive*, stable-CIDR answer. **This is the VPC's one non-premature job** —
176	   exclusivity, distinct from the privacy boundaries A–D, which the MVP already covers.
177	2. you need to hide network metadata (that you talk to Bedrock at all), or
178	3. you want private paths to *several* AWS services and want to amortize the peer.
179	
180	Until then the Outpost is premature — and it spends the "no instance to secure" win, since a
181	private tunnel into a VPC requires a standing termination point (the peer or a pricier managed
182	VPN; there is no serverless inbound tunnel).
183	
184	## 8. Cost
185	
186	The Outpost's *always-on* floor (accrues whether or not anyone infers):
187	
188	| Component | ~Monthly |
189	|---|---|
190	| EC2 `t3.nano` peer | $3.80 |
191	| Elastic IP (1 public IPv4) | $3.65 (possibly ~$0 under the 750-hr in-use allowance) |
192	| PrivateLink interface endpoint (1 AZ) | $7.30 |
193	| Data processing (text payloads) | pennies |
194	| VPC / subnets / route tables / security groups | free |
195	| **Total (single-AZ)** | **≈ $15** |
196	
197	Dual-AZ for High Availability (HA = a redundant copy in a second Availability Zone, so one
198	datacenter outage doesn't kill the path) adds ~$7/mo. **Skip HA** for a solo/small private
199	path; reconnect when the AZ recovers.
200	
201	The **one** cost to actively avoid is a **NAT gateway** (~$32/mo + data). The design routes
202	through the peer and never needs one; keep it out of the provisioning templates. (A NAT
203	gateway is for private subnets reaching the *public* internet — which this design never does.)
204	
205	Comparison: Claude on Bedrock is Sonnet **$3/$15** and Opus **$5/$25** per million in/out
206	tokens (+~10% cross-region; caching up to 90% off cached input). The entire ~$15/mo floor ≈ 1
207	million Sonnet output tokens ≈ **one active user's day or two** of real work. At any genuine
208	usage the conduit hosting is rounding error. **Optimize the Outpost for correctness and
209	security, not for its hosting cost.**
210	
211	## 9. Horizon — JJK-choreographed multi-Quire interactions
212	
213	This is the forward vision that shapes the present design without being built here.
214	
215	Future Job-Jockey-choreographed LLM interactions enlist *several Quires of different natures*
216	— different models, different `agency` levels, different residency — across one tightly
217	managed conversation. A private-counsel Quire (Opus, `agency: none`), a research Quire
218	(`agency: internet`), a fast-triage Quire (Haiku, `none`); an orchestrator routes among them.
219	This is the argument for a Quire being a **typed, governed object**, not a thin model pointer.
220	
221	**"A Quire identity enables this," grounded.** A Quire's identity is, concretely, its
222	**governed invocation principal** — the IAM role/policy that encodes its nature (model access,
223	agency ceiling, residency, capability). Enlisting a Quire = using its principal. The identity
224	is not person-like; it is a *named policy bundle* that makes Quires addressable and governable
225	in a choreography rather than merely callable.
226	
227	**The central open question: is a Quire an *instance* or a *menu*?**
228	
229	- *Instance* framing: a Quire is one concrete configured endpoint; "different natures" means
230	  several Quires; choreography routes among them.
231	- *Menu* framing (the operator's lean, and the more promising one): a Quire is a declaration
232	  of *what is allowed in the current situation* — an envelope of permitted (model, agency,
233	  cost/availability) combinations. The consumer (JJK) **subselects** a concrete invocation
234	  from the menu at use-time.
235	
236	The menu framing yields a clean division of labor that matches everything else in this memo:
237	**Recipe Bottle governs the envelope — approvals, allowances, availability, cost management —
238	and the consumer picks within it.** Under it, the Quire's fields become *ceilings and
239	constraints* rather than fixed values: `agency` is the *maximum* permitted, region is a
240	*constraint*, model is a *list of approved options*, cost is an *allowance/cap*; a
241	subselection must fall within. This also strengthens §2 — what Recipe Bottle persists is
242	precisely the *menu* (the governed, stateless-side-absent envelope), and the concrete
243	invocation stays ephemeral, mirroring Bedrock's own statelessness. It is offered as the
244	candidate resolution, **not decreed**; settling instance-vs-menu (or a both-at-different-
245	altitudes synthesis) is deferred.
246	
247	**Privacy becomes a graph property.** The deepest consequence: in a multi-Quire choreography,
248	confidentiality no longer lives in the nodes. If a private Quire's output (`agency: none`)
249	flows into an agentic Quire's input (`agency: internet`), the private data can exfiltrate
250	**through the sibling's agency** — and no per-Quire guarantee catches it, because each Quire
251	is individually well-behaved. So "tightly managed conversations" must carry **information-flow
252	rules** — which Quire's output may feed which Quire's input — and the confidentiality lives in
253	the *edges* of the conversation graph.
254	
255	Elegantly, the `agency` lattice (`none < internal < internet`) **doubles as the clearance
256	lattice** for that flow control: *output from a lower-agency Quire may not flow into a
257	higher-agency Quire without an explicit declassification step.* The field added for the MVP is
258	the same primitive the future choreography needs — a sign the shape is right.
259	
260	The orchestration itself is a separate future effort (JJK-adjacent) and must not touch the
261	MVP. Recorded here as a shaping force and one named hazard, nothing more.
262	
263	## 10. Open / deferred
264	
265	- **Prefix minting** — `RBRQ` candidate for the Quire regime; Outpost needs a non-`RBRO` slot
266	  (RBRO taken by OAuth). Deferred to a minting pass. (The Census/Crucible `C` collision once
267	  parked here is resolved: Census → Terrier.)
268	- **Instance vs menu** — §9's central question; the menu/envelope resolution is the candidate.
269	- **Cross-Quire information flow** — the graph-property hazard; the agency lattice is its
270	  clearance lattice. Belongs to the choreography effort, not here.
271	- **Identity upgrade** — confined-credential MVP → federated STS (envoy) fast-follow; rides
272	  ₣BZ landing federation.
273	- **HA** — skip for the private path.
274	- **Agentic surfaces** — a non-goal for the private Quire unless deliberately re-opened (and
275	  then governed by IAM, never the sentry).
276	- **Roadmap correction** — RBSHR's "VPC Service Controls perimeter" entry cross-references the
277	  conduit VPC as shareable with the VPC-SC perimeter "to serve both consumers." That reads as
278	  literal sharing and does not hold: VPC-SC is a *GCP* service perimeter around the depot
279	  project; the Outpost VPC is an *AWS* network. Different clouds; neither shares. Fix the
280	  cross-reference when RBSHR's conduit/chantry stubs get breadcrumbed to this memo.
281	- **Terminus consumer class** — §11's open question: container-only consumers (plain
282	  container networking) vs. a host-resident consumer (needs a local forward-proxy / endpoint
283	  seam from the terminus, still no host VPN). Container-only is the lean; unsettled.
284	
285	## 11. Launch shape — the Outpost's local terminus
286	
287	A same-session addendum extending §5 and §7. The shaping above left one thing unsaid: the
288	Outpost is "peer-bearing" on the *AWS* side, but where does the tunnel's *local* end live?
289	Three candidate homes were weighed; two are rejected here and the third adopted as the working
290	shape.
291	
292	**Not in the sentry.** Folding the tunnel client into the sentry inverts the sentry's purpose.
293	The sentry is a *containment-harness* component — it jails a possibly-hostile bottle and exists
294	to prove the jail holds (the Ifrit/tadmor stance: the inside is the threat). A tunnel terminus
295	serving a *trusted* reach is the opposite stance — trusted infrastructure, not a contained
296	prisoner. Crossing the two makes the sentry mean two contradictory things at once. The
297	predecessor's answer was WireGuard-in-sentry (the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, §12); it is rejected
298	here.
299	
300	**Not a bottle-less crucible either.** The tempting shortcut — a crucible charged with no
301	bottle — is a category error. The crucible *is* the containment harness; a bottle-less crucible
302	is a jail with no prisoner, and hollowing out its one purpose to borrow its lifecycle plumbing
303	is abstraction strain, not a feature.
304	
305	**The factoring that holds.** The image ecosystem (vessel → hallmark → nameplate) is separable
306	from the runtime harness (crucible). The crucible is one *consumer* of hallmark images. The
307	local terminus is a **second launch shape** beside it — same build pipeline, different runtime —
308	not a variant of the first. "A whole different notion of launched container" is the correct
309	read, and the signal that it belongs beside the crucible, not inside it.
310	
311	**Its home in the vocabulary: the Outpost's local end.** The terminus is the client-side
312	counterpart of the EC2 peer — built as its own vessel, launched standalone. The Outpost now has
313	two ends (AWS peer + local terminus), both operated by `raise`/`strike`; no new verbs, no new
314	top-level concept. This finishes the migration this memo began: away from WireGuard-in-sentry,
315	toward a dedicated terminus.
316	
317	**"No host WireGuard" is a design constraint, and the terminus honors it.** The tunnel client
318	lives in a *container*; its config rides inside the hallmark through the same pipeline as
319	everything else, and the workstation host never joins a VPN. That is the "path based on the
320	container image ecosystem" — literally.
321	
322	**MVP untouched.** The terminus is fast-follow only: it exists only once there is an Outpost to
323	terminate. The MVP (allowlist reach, sentry egress-lockdown, no Outpost, no VPC) is unaffected,
324	and there the sentry keeps doing exactly what it does today. This addendum adds no MVP surface —
325	consistent with §9's rule that the forward vision must not touch the MVP.
326	
327	**One open question, named not solved (§10): how a consumer reaches the terminus.** Container
328	consumers reach it by plain container networking — trivial, never touching the host. A
329	host-resident consumer (operator tooling outside any container) would need the terminus to
330	expose a local seam — most plausibly a forward proxy / endpoint-override the SDK points at —
331	*still* no host VPN. Which consumer class is in scope changes the design; container-only is
332	dramatically simpler and is the current lean.
333	
334	## 12. Sources
335	
336	- `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` — the predecessor (approaches,
337	  WireGuard-in-sentry, service-compatibility survey, threat-model table).
338	- RBSHR Horizon Roadmap — the `chantry`, `conduit`, `embassy`/`envoy`/`bullion`, and
339	  `VPC Service Controls` entries (stubs; to be breadcrumbed to this memo).
340	- Heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`) — the citizen/capability identity model this rides;
341	  intent-vs-enforcement and the Terrier.
342	- AWS, verified 2026-06-09: Bedrock is serverless/stateless and ZDR-by-default
343	  (data-protection docs); provider isolation via dedicated deployment accounts; model
344	  invocation logging opt-in/off; abuse detection automated/no-human-review; Claude on Bedrock
345	  under Commercial Terms (no training); cross-region inference residency (geographic vs
346	  global); plain inference has no network reach while AgentCore Browser/Code-Interpreter and
347	  Lambda action groups execute server-side and can reach the internet; pricing — Sonnet
348	  $3/$15, Opus $5/$25 per M tokens, PrivateLink endpoint ~$0.01/hr/AZ, t3.nano ~$3.80/mo,
349	  public IPv4 ~$3.65/mo, NAT gateway ~$0.045/hr.
350

# Bedrock Quire — Consume-Side Shaping

Date: 2026-06-09

Status: intent/shaping. Nothing here is built. This memo records the *shape* of a
future Recipe Bottle subsystem for safely consuming frontier models on AWS Bedrock,
so the durable conclusions survive into the roadmap and a later build. It supersedes
nothing; it **extends** `memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (which predates
the identity model and the consume/host split) and **rides** the citizen/capability
identity model of heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`).

This is a *successor* to the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, not an edit of it — that memo is a
dated snapshot and keeps its provenance. Where the two disagree, this one is current.

---

## 1. Scope — consume, not host

The need is **safe, private use of frontier models** (Claude and peers on Bedrock) for the
operator and the operator's users. Conversations must stay private. That is the whole point.

Explicitly **in scope**: invoking *native foundation models already on Bedrock* from a
contained client.

Explicitly **out of scope** (collapses away because we are not hosting our own weights):
the embassy / bullion / entrust / Custom-Model-Import delivery path. We are not pushing
models to AWS. The roadmap's `chantry` entry imagined both a custom-imported model and a
native one; only the native-consume half lives here.

A consequence worth stating plainly up front (it corrects the operator's initial mental
model): **Bedrock is not an "instance" you run and keep secure.** It is a fully managed,
serverless, *stateless* request/response API. There is no box to harden, and — per Recipe
Bottle's **asymmetric-sovereignty** stance — no AWS principal Recipe Bottle owns. What you
control is: which model you invoke, the IAM perimeter, the network path, the logging switch,
and the AWS account itself. "Secure within Recipe Bottle" therefore splits: the *consuming*
end is secured inside a bottle; the *serving* end is yours in AWS, stood up from
Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates but owned by you wearing an AWS hat.

## 2. Why Recipe Bottle holds the persistent state

Bedrock is stateless: each call is independent, and even the conversation history is held by
the *client* and re-sent every turn. So the durable truth of "which model, reached how, under
whose identity, at what cost ceiling" has **nowhere to live on the Bedrock side.** That is the
argument for a persistent Recipe Bottle config model: statelessness over there forces
persistence over here.

## 3. The privacy model — six boundaries, one Palisade

"Completely private" decomposes into distinct boundaries with distinct strengths. The first
four can be driven to near-airtight; the last two are the honest ceiling.

- **A — From the public internet (network).** PrivateLink keeps traffic on the AWS network;
  allowlist + TLS is the public-path fallback. Strong; delivered client-side by the conduit.
- **B — From the model provider.** Architectural: Bedrock runs each provider's model in an
  isolated deployment account the provider cannot reach; zero provider operator access. Under
  Commercial Terms (which Bedrock is), the provider does not train on your data. Strong.
- **C — From your own logs.** Bedrock defaults to zero-data-retention; model-invocation
  logging is opt-in, off by default. The main self-inflicted leak is turning logging on and
  under-securing the sink. Default: leave it off.
- **D — From AWS retention/training/sharing.** Contractual + ZDR; abuse detection is fully
  automated, no human review, no storage (verify per model — a few carry a 30-day
  classifier-flagged exception; Claude is in the clean automated-only camp).
- **E — From AWS hardware at the moment of inference.** The boundary **no hosted frontier
  model can cross.** The model must read your plaintext to answer; AWS silicon processes
  cleartext. The guarantee is *contractual + architectural + compliance-audited* — genuinely
  strong, the same class of trust under all enterprise cloud — but trust *placed*, not trust
  *eliminated*. If the threat model requires "the operator mathematically cannot see it,"
  nothing hosted meets that.
- **F — From server-side agency egress (NEW).** Plain inference (`Converse`/`InvokeModel`)
  has *zero* network reach — the model is a pure prompt→completion function and even a tool
  *decision* never touches the wire. But agentic surfaces — Bedrock Agents with Lambda action
  groups, and AgentCore Browser / Code Interpreter / the server-side tool execution of the
  Responses API — execute **server-side** and can reach the open internet **behind the
  Palisade, entirely outside the sentry's view.** This is opt-in (a different, more complex
  API surface; you never get it by accident), and the **only** lever over it is **IAM**, not
  the sentry.

**The Palisade.** Boundaries A, C, and the client side of B/D sit inside our realm and get
full sovereignty. E is irreducible foreign ground — characterize and contain, do not pretend
to govern. F is the subtle one: the sentry governs the *bottle's* egress and is structurally
blind to *Bedrock's* server-side agency, so F is governed only by IAM and by *not invoking
agentic surfaces*. The private Quire uses non-agentic surfaces only; agentic Bedrock is a
deliberate, named re-opening of the egress question on AWS's side of the Pale.

## 4. What Recipe Bottle does — and does not — do

Recipe Bottle **establishes and maintains the binding**: the config, the identity, the reach,
the cost envelope. The **inference calls are made by whatever runs inside the bottle** (an
app, a chat client, an orchestrator) — *not* by Recipe Bottle. There is therefore **no
"submit" verb**. Recipe Bottle's surface ends at "the path and identity are ready and
verified." The only place Recipe Bottle itself touches Bedrock is a one-shot *probe* (does it
answer?), a health check, not a workload call.

This single rule shapes the whole vocabulary: the verbs are *lifecycle of the binding*, never
invocation.

## 5. Vocabulary

The roadmap's working words `chantry` (target) and `conduit` (reach) both start with `C`,
which stumbles against Crucible. Renamed to unbound primary letters:

| Concept | Noun | Verbs | Register | Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| The model endpoint a bottle consumes | **Quire** | `endow` / `dissolve` | ecclesiastical (heir to *chantry*'s "where the voice issues") | 0..N |
| The peer-bearing private reach into an AWS VPC | **Outpost** | `raise` / `strike` | camp/diplomatic (sibling to *embassy*) | 0..1 |

All four verbs are collision-free across RB and JJK today (`grep` lands clean — the
findability rationale the operator asked us to favor).

**Two tiers of lifecycle.** The Outpost is the heavy, shared, singleton reach (raise once,
strike once). Quires are the light bindings that ride it (endow/dissolve freely). This is the
conduit-vs-target split, resolved: the reach object is first-class, not footprint — its 0..1
cardinality is exactly what earns it a noun and its own verbs.

**The peer is *not* separately named.** It is the Outpost's compute; `raise`/`strike` operate
on the Outpost, which includes the peer. No peer-lifecycle verbs — the peer is hourly-cheap,
stop/start saves only noise, and the endpoint/EIP are standing anyway.

**Prefix allocation (open minting item).** A Quire regime is a natural sibling to
nameplate/vessel; `RBRQ` is a free slot in the `RBR*` family. The Outpost cannot be `RBRO`
(taken by the OAuth regime), so its config home needs a non-`RBRO` slot — deferred to the
later minting pass.

## 6. Config shape — three orthogonal layers

A "Bedrock configuration" is not one blob; it is three independently-variable layers (same
model reachable two ways; same reach under two identities; same identity to three models).
They want composable homes, not a monolith.

- **Target — the Quire.** Fields: model(s); version-pin (Bedrock model IDs drift — pin for
  provenance, as we pin everything else); region + inference profile (single-region or
  geographic, **not global** by default — a silent residency/privacy knob); **`agency`** (see
  below); a capability reference (who may invoke); a reach selector; logging posture
  (`off` by default).
- **Reach — `allowlist` or `outpost`.** This extends the nameplate's network posture. The
  MVP value is `allowlist` with **no Outpost in existence**; the fast-follow raises an Outpost
  and repoints. Egress allowlist scope for a private Quire: the Bedrock endpoint, plus the STS
  endpoint if federating, and **nothing else** — the egress-lockdown crown jewel.
- **Identity — the invoking principal.** MVP: a confined credential placed in the bottle
  (RBRA-style regime), because ₣BZ's federation is future. Fast-follow: federated short-lived
  STS (the envoy pattern), no resting key. Either way the identity is decoupled from
  capability per ₣BZ.

**The `agency` field (boundary F's declared home).** A lattice, not a boolean:

- `none` — plain inference, no server-side tools. (privacy-max; the MVP default and the only
  MVP value)
- `internal` — server-side tools, but only your resources (e.g. a knowledge base over your
  data); no internet.
- `internet` — server-side tools that reach the open web (AgentCore Browser, etc.).
  (privacy-off)

`agency` is *declared intent that IAM enforces* — the same intent-vs-enforcement spine as
₣BZ. The Quire's invocation principal carries an agency clause; an audit can diff declared vs.
actual IAM. Even though every MVP Quire is `agency: none`, declaring it earns its keep: it
makes the privacy guarantee an explicit, enforced property rather than an absence, and gives
the IAM *denial* a documented home.

**Note the two outward directions, kept distinct.** `reach` is how the *bottle* gets *in* to
Bedrock (sentry-governed). `agency` is how far the *model* may act *out* (IAM-governed). Same
Quire, opposite directions, one field each — do not let one blur into the other.

## 7. Staging

**MVP** — allowlist Quire, single Quire, `agency: none`, plain inference, confined credential
in the bottle, no Outpost, no VPC, no standing AWS infrastructure. This already delivers the
privacy story: boundaries A (TLS + egress-lockdown), B, C, D hold; the bottle excludes the
workstation from the data path and can leak nowhere but Bedrock. **The VPC is not what keeps
conversations private — this MVP already does.**

**Fast-follow** — raise an Outpost (PrivateLink), repoint Quires to `reach: outpost`, and
upgrade identity to federation. Build the VPC only when one of three triggers fires:
1. egress **exclusivity** is required — allowlisting Bedrock by static CIDR is ≈ "all of
   Amazon" (no dedicated published range); domain/SNI is tighter but shares IPs; PrivateLink
   is the one *exclusive*, stable-CIDR answer. **This is the VPC's one non-premature job** —
   exclusivity, distinct from the privacy boundaries A–D, which the MVP already covers.
2. you need to hide network metadata (that you talk to Bedrock at all), or
3. you want private paths to *several* AWS services and want to amortize the peer.

Until then the Outpost is premature — and it spends the "no instance to secure" win, since a
private tunnel into a VPC requires a standing termination point (the peer or a pricier managed
VPN; there is no serverless inbound tunnel).

## 8. Cost

The Outpost's *always-on* floor (accrues whether or not anyone infers):

| Component | ~Monthly |
|---|---|
| EC2 `t3.nano` peer | $3.80 |
| Elastic IP (1 public IPv4) | $3.65 (possibly ~$0 under the 750-hr in-use allowance) |
| PrivateLink interface endpoint (1 AZ) | $7.30 |
| Data processing (text payloads) | pennies |
| VPC / subnets / route tables / security groups | free |
| **Total (single-AZ)** | **≈ $15** |

Dual-AZ for High Availability (HA = a redundant copy in a second Availability Zone, so one
datacenter outage doesn't kill the path) adds ~$7/mo. **Skip HA** for a solo/small private
path; reconnect when the AZ recovers.

The **one** cost to actively avoid is a **NAT gateway** (~$32/mo + data). The design routes
through the peer and never needs one; keep it out of the provisioning templates. (A NAT
gateway is for private subnets reaching the *public* internet — which this design never does.)

Comparison: Claude on Bedrock is Sonnet **$3/$15** and Opus **$5/$25** per million in/out
tokens (+~10% cross-region; caching up to 90% off cached input). The entire ~$15/mo floor ≈ 1
million Sonnet output tokens ≈ **one active user's day or two** of real work. At any genuine
usage the conduit hosting is rounding error. **Optimize the Outpost for correctness and
security, not for its hosting cost.**

## 9. Horizon — JJK-choreographed multi-Quire interactions

This is the forward vision that shapes the present design without being built here.

Future Job-Jockey-choreographed LLM interactions enlist *several Quires of different natures*
— different models, different `agency` levels, different residency — across one tightly
managed conversation. A private-counsel Quire (Opus, `agency: none`), a research Quire
(`agency: internet`), a fast-triage Quire (Haiku, `none`); an orchestrator routes among them.
This is the argument for a Quire being a **typed, governed object**, not a thin model pointer.

**"A Quire identity enables this," grounded.** A Quire's identity is, concretely, its
**governed invocation principal** — the IAM role/policy that encodes its nature (model access,
agency ceiling, residency, capability). Enlisting a Quire = using its principal. The identity
is not person-like; it is a *named policy bundle* that makes Quires addressable and governable
in a choreography rather than merely callable.

**The central open question: is a Quire an *instance* or a *menu*?**

- *Instance* framing: a Quire is one concrete configured endpoint; "different natures" means
  several Quires; choreography routes among them.
- *Menu* framing (the operator's lean, and the more promising one): a Quire is a declaration
  of *what is allowed in the current situation* — an envelope of permitted (model, agency,
  cost/availability) combinations. The consumer (JJK) **subselects** a concrete invocation
  from the menu at use-time.

The menu framing yields a clean division of labor that matches everything else in this memo:
**Recipe Bottle governs the envelope — approvals, allowances, availability, cost management —
and the consumer picks within it.** Under it, the Quire's fields become *ceilings and
constraints* rather than fixed values: `agency` is the *maximum* permitted, region is a
*constraint*, model is a *list of approved options*, cost is an *allowance/cap*; a
subselection must fall within. This also strengthens §2 — what Recipe Bottle persists is
precisely the *menu* (the governed, stateless-side-absent envelope), and the concrete
invocation stays ephemeral, mirroring Bedrock's own statelessness. It is offered as the
candidate resolution, **not decreed**; settling instance-vs-menu (or a both-at-different-
altitudes synthesis) is deferred.

**Privacy becomes a graph property.** The deepest consequence: in a multi-Quire choreography,
confidentiality no longer lives in the nodes. If a private Quire's output (`agency: none`)
flows into an agentic Quire's input (`agency: internet`), the private data can exfiltrate
**through the sibling's agency** — and no per-Quire guarantee catches it, because each Quire
is individually well-behaved. So "tightly managed conversations" must carry **information-flow
rules** — which Quire's output may feed which Quire's input — and the confidentiality lives in
the *edges* of the conversation graph.

Elegantly, the `agency` lattice (`none < internal < internet`) **doubles as the clearance
lattice** for that flow control: *output from a lower-agency Quire may not flow into a
higher-agency Quire without an explicit declassification step.* The field added for the MVP is
the same primitive the future choreography needs — a sign the shape is right.

The orchestration itself is a separate future effort (JJK-adjacent) and must not touch the
MVP. Recorded here as a shaping force and one named hazard, nothing more.

## 10. Open / deferred

- **Prefix minting** — `RBRQ` candidate for the Quire regime; Outpost needs a non-`RBRO` slot
  (RBRO taken by OAuth). Deferred to a minting pass. (The Census/Crucible `C` collision once
  parked here is resolved: Census → Terrier.)
- **Instance vs menu** — §9's central question; the menu/envelope resolution is the candidate.
- **Cross-Quire information flow** — the graph-property hazard; the agency lattice is its
  clearance lattice. Belongs to the choreography effort, not here.
- **Identity upgrade** — confined-credential MVP → federated STS (envoy) fast-follow; rides
  ₣BZ landing federation.
- **HA** — skip for the private path.
- **Agentic surfaces** — a non-goal for the private Quire unless deliberately re-opened (and
  then governed by IAM, never the sentry).
- **Roadmap correction** — RBSHR's "VPC Service Controls perimeter" entry cross-references the
  conduit VPC as shareable with the VPC-SC perimeter "to serve both consumers." That reads as
  literal sharing and does not hold: VPC-SC is a *GCP* service perimeter around the depot
  project; the Outpost VPC is an *AWS* network. Different clouds; neither shares. Fix the
  cross-reference when RBSHR's conduit/chantry stubs get breadcrumbed to this memo.
- **Terminus consumer class** — §11's open question: container-only consumers (plain
  container networking) vs. a host-resident consumer (needs a local forward-proxy / endpoint
  seam from the terminus, still no host VPN). Container-only is the lean; unsettled.

## 11. Launch shape — the Outpost's local terminus

A same-session addendum extending §5 and §7. The shaping above left one thing unsaid: the
Outpost is "peer-bearing" on the *AWS* side, but where does the tunnel's *local* end live?
Three candidate homes were weighed; two are rejected here and the third adopted as the working
shape.

**Not in the sentry.** Folding the tunnel client into the sentry inverts the sentry's purpose.
The sentry is a *containment-harness* component — it jails a possibly-hostile bottle and exists
to prove the jail holds (the Ifrit/tadmor stance: the inside is the threat). A tunnel terminus
serving a *trusted* reach is the opposite stance — trusted infrastructure, not a contained
prisoner. Crossing the two makes the sentry mean two contradictory things at once. The
predecessor's answer was WireGuard-in-sentry (the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, §12); it is rejected
here.

**Not a bottle-less crucible either.** The tempting shortcut — a crucible charged with no
bottle — is a category error. The crucible *is* the containment harness; a bottle-less crucible
is a jail with no prisoner, and hollowing out its one purpose to borrow its lifecycle plumbing
is abstraction strain, not a feature.

**The factoring that holds.** The image ecosystem (vessel → hallmark → nameplate) is separable
from the runtime harness (crucible). The crucible is one *consumer* of hallmark images. The
local terminus is a **second launch shape** beside it — same build pipeline, different runtime —
not a variant of the first. "A whole different notion of launched container" is the correct
read, and the signal that it belongs beside the crucible, not inside it.

**Its home in the vocabulary: the Outpost's local end.** The terminus is the client-side
counterpart of the EC2 peer — built as its own vessel, launched standalone. The Outpost now has
two ends (AWS peer + local terminus), both operated by `raise`/`strike`; no new verbs, no new
top-level concept. This finishes the migration this memo began: away from WireGuard-in-sentry,
toward a dedicated terminus.

**"No host WireGuard" is a design constraint, and the terminus honors it.** The tunnel client
lives in a *container*; its config rides inside the hallmark through the same pipeline as
everything else, and the workstation host never joins a VPN. That is the "path based on the
container image ecosystem" — literally.

**MVP untouched.** The terminus is fast-follow only: it exists only once there is an Outpost to
terminate. The MVP (allowlist reach, sentry egress-lockdown, no Outpost, no VPC) is unaffected,
and there the sentry keeps doing exactly what it does today. This addendum adds no MVP surface —
consistent with §9's rule that the forward vision must not touch the MVP.

**One open question, named not solved (§10): how a consumer reaches the terminus.** Container
consumers reach it by plain container networking — trivial, never touching the host. A
host-resident consumer (operator tooling outside any container) would need the terminus to
expose a local seam — most plausibly a forward proxy / endpoint-override the SDK points at —
*still* no host VPN. Which consumer class is in scope changes the design; container-only is
dramatically simpler and is the current lean.

## 12. Sources

- `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` — the predecessor (approaches,
  WireGuard-in-sentry, service-compatibility survey, threat-model table).
- RBSHR Horizon Roadmap — the `chantry`, `conduit`, `embassy`/`envoy`/`bullion`, and
  `VPC Service Controls` entries (stubs; to be breadcrumbed to this memo).
- Heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`) — the citizen/capability identity model this rides;
  intent-vs-enforcement and the Terrier.
- AWS, verified 2026-06-09: Bedrock is serverless/stateless and ZDR-by-default
  (data-protection docs); provider isolation via dedicated deployment accounts; model
  invocation logging opt-in/off; abuse detection automated/no-human-review; Claude on Bedrock
  under Commercial Terms (no training); cross-region inference residency (geographic vs
  global); plain inference has no network reach while AgentCore Browser/Code-Interpreter and
  Lambda action groups execute server-side and can reach the internet; pricing — Sonnet
  $3/$15, Opus $5/$25 per M tokens, PrivateLink endpoint ~$0.01/hr/AZ, t3.nano ~$3.80/mo,
  public IPv4 ~$3.65/mo, NAT gateway ~$0.045/hr.

582	
583	// ============================================================================
584	// TIER 1: WHY AND WHAT
585	// ============================================================================
586	
587	== System Overview
588	
589	=== Core Trust Challenge
590	
591	Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
592	{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.
593	
594	This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:
595	
596	*
597	{st_container_s}
598	provide powerful tool isolation
599	* But they need controlled access to do useful work
600	* Standard
601	{st_container}
602	networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.
603	
604	=== The Bottle Pattern
605	
606	{at_rbm_system}
607	solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
608	{st_container}
609	type here referred to as a
610	{at_bottle_container}:
611	
612	. File Access Boundary
613	*
614	{at_bottle_container}
615	sees only explicitly controlled set of
616	{st_volume_mount_s}
617	* No other access to
618	{at_workstation}
619	filesystem
620	
621	. Network Boundary
622	*
623	{at_bottle_container}
624	has no direct network access
625	* All traffic flows through dedicated
626	{at_sentry_container},
627	a
628	{st_container}
629	configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
630	* Only declared
631	{st_port_map_s}
632	and destinations allowed
633	* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic
634	
635	Each
636	{rbsc_crucible}
637	maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:
638	
639	* One
640	{at_sentry_container}
641	enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
642	* One
643	{at_pentacle_container}
644	establishing and configuring the network namespace
645	* One
646	{at_bottle_container}
647	running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
648	* Clear configuration in a single
649	{at_nameplate}
650	file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts
651	
652	Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
653	A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
654	{at_sentry_container}
655	must be healthy before the
656	{at_pentacle_container}
657	starts, and the
658	{at_pentacle_container}
659	must be healthy before the
660	{at_bottle_container}
661	starts.
662	This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
663	The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
664	Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
665	{at_recipe_bottle}
666	Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
667	Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
668	Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).
669	
670	The
671	{at_pentacle_container}
672	is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
673	{at_sentry_container},
674	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
675	{at_bottle_container}
676	via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
677	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
678	{at_bottle_container}
679	experiences only a functional path to its
680	{at_sentry_container}
681	gateway
682	
683	=== Security Properties
684	
685	The
686	{at_rbm_system}
687	maintains following essential guarantees:
688	
689	. File Access Control
690	*
691	{at_bottle_container_s}
692	can access only explicitly configured
693	{st_volume_mount_s}
694	* No access to broader system resources
695	
696	. Network Isolation
697	* All traffic flows through dedicated
698	{at_sentry_container}
699	* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
700	* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
701	* Only explicitly configured
702	{st_port_map_s}
703	allow traffic between
704	{at_transit_network}
705	and
706	{at_enclave_network}
707	*
708	{at_bottle_container_s}
709	have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
710	{at_sentry_container_s}
711	* Comprehensive traffic logging
712	
713	. Service Separation
714	* Each
715	{rbsc_crucible}
716	fully isolated
717	* No cross-service communication
718	* Independent security policies
719	* Clean state separation
720	
721	. Service Access Control
722	* External access to services only permitted when
723	{rbrn_entry_mode}
724	is enabled
725	* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
726	* All service access must originate from outside the pod
727	* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
728	{at_sentry_container}
729	->
730	{at_bottle_container}
731	
732	These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
733	They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
734	{at_bottle_container}
735	(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
736	Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.
737	
738	=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit
739	
740	include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]
741	
742	== Getting Started
743	
744	For builders and operators new to
745	{at_recipe_bottle},
746	this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.
747	
748	include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]
749	
750	// ============================================================================
751	// TIER 2: HOW
752	// ============================================================================
753	
754	== Google API Engagement
755	
756	{at_recipe_bottle}
757	invokes
758	{gcp_service}
759	APIs through
760	{giam_service_account}
761	JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.
762	
763	* *Authentication*:
764	{giam_service_account_s}
765	use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
766	These JWTs are exchanged for
767	{oauth_token_s}
768	via Google's token endpoint.
769	All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.
770	
771	* *Invocation Pattern*:
772	Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
773	Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
774	Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.
775	
776	* *Long-Running Operations*:
777	{gcp_service}
778	{gcp_lro_s}
779	return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
780	The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
781	The system handles both success and error states.
782	Operations utilize
783	{rbbc_submit}
784	and
785	{rbbc_await}
786	to handle all asynchronous operations.
787	
788	* *IAM Policies*:
789	All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
790	{gcp_service}
791	resources.
792	
793	* *IAM Member Format*:
794	All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
795	+
796	GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
797	The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
798	`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
799	+
800	Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
801	The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
802	+
803	Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.
804	
805	== Operational Invariants
806	
807	=== Concurrent Session Safety
808	
809	{at_recipe_bottle}
810	commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
811	This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.
812	
813	Concurrency safety is achieved through:
814	
815	* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
816	Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.
817	
818	* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
819	Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
820	{rbtgi_repository}.
821	
822	* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
823	Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
824	The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.
825	
826	* *Semantic Preconditions*:
827	Operations like
828	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
829	require clean git state.
830	This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.
831	
832	NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
833	Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.
834	
835	NOTE: The
836	{gcb_service}
837	private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
838	Machine type is set at pool creation time via
839	{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
840	(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
841	If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
842	build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
843	The
844	{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
845	regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
846	See
847	{rbtgo_quota_build}
848	for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.
849	
850	== Key Premises
851	
852	[[rbsk_no_gcloud]]
853	//axl_voices axk_premise
854	{rbsk_no_gcloud}::
855	{at_recipe_bottle}
856	scripts MUST NOT invoke the `gcloud` CLI in local bash scripts or transitively through helper scripts.
857	All local
858	{gcp_service}
859	interaction uses direct REST API calls via `curl` with JWT-authenticated bearer tokens.
860	+
861	**Exception — vouch provenance fetch**: The {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verification step (conjure mode only) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` for SLSA provenance envelope retrieval.
862	This wraps complex Container Analysis API interaction that would be burdensome to replicate via raw REST.
863	All other GCB step operations use the GCE metadata server for OAuth token acquisition and direct registry REST API calls — no gcloud.
864	The provenance fetch runs as Python 3 on the gcloud builder image after the primary image is built; the reproducibility concern that motivates this premise is weaker for post-build verification.
865	Works under NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
866	+
867	In GCB build step contexts (conjure, bind, graft, enshrine), registry authentication uses the GCE metadata server to obtain an access token and writes a static Docker config JSON.
868	+
869	Rationale: `gcloud` is an opaque, version-sensitive binary that obscures API semantics, introduces implicit authentication state, and breaks reproducibility.
870	The `gcloud auth configure-docker` approach also fails mechanically in GCB steps — the gcloud builder image lacks `docker` in PATH, so the credential helper it installs cannot function.
871	+
872	NOTE: The gcloud reliquary image exists because every ordain-path build step (conjure, bind, graft) uses it for the about/vouch pipeline (`bash`, `date`, and `gcloud` for provenance retrieval per the exception above).
873	The reliquary toolchain is mode-uniform: all ordain modes consume gcloud, docker, alpine, and syft from the reliquary; bind additionally uses skopeo for image-copy.
874	
875	[[rbsk_in_pool_preflight]]
876	//axl_voices axk_premise
877	{rbsk_in_pool_preflight}::
878	Every ordain-path Cloud Build job — conjure's combined image+about, bind's combined mirror+about, graft's combined about+vouch, and enshrine — prepends a step 0 (`rbgjr01-reliquary-preflight.sh`) that HEAD-checks reliquary tool images from the worker pool's vantage before expensive work runs.
879	Conjure additionally HEAD-checks the vessel's enshrined base anchors (slots 1-3 from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR`); other modes pass empty locator substitutions.
880	+
881	Coverage is 5 of 6 reliquary tools — gcloud, docker, syft, binfmt, skopeo.
882	Alpine, the preflight step's own image, is validated implicitly by Cloud Build pulling it before the script runs.
883	Failure produces a structured "this pool can't see «TOOL»" message naming each missed item, with remediation directive (re-inscribe + re-yoke + re-ordain).
884	+
885	Rationale: defense-in-depth on top of the host-side `zrbfd_registry_preflight` (which validates the Director's host-side view of GAR).
886	The in-pool check catches divergence the host-side check cannot — airgap private-VPC routing, Director↔Mason IAM split, and submit↔execute time skew.
887	+
888	Scope boundary: standalone vouch (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`) and standalone about (`zrbfv_about_submit`) are operator-recovery paths and are not currently covered.
889	
890	[[rbsk_invest_serialized]]
891	//axl_voices axk_premise
892	{rbsk_invest_serialized}::
893	{rbtgo_retriever_invest}, {rbtgo_director_invest}, and {rbtgo_governor_mantle} write credential bytes to a singleton assay slot (`RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE`), not a per-identity slot.
894	The operator MUST move `RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE` to a safe place before initiating the next operation that writes to the assay slot — typically the role's production slot (`RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE`, `RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE`, or `RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE`) when the invester and the consumer are the same operator, otherwise a delivery channel to the consumer.
895	Two consecutive assay-slot writers without an intervening move lose the first writer's bytes.
896	Batched invest is not supported — by design.
897	+
898	Rationale: a singleton assay slot keeps the producer-output discipline uniform across roles and frees the implementation from path-space per-identity routing.
899	The operator-driven move step is the deliberate promotion gate from producer output to role-production consumer; it also acts as the operator's explicit acknowledgement of which identity owns the role at runtime, since `RBRA` files encode exactly one identity at a time.
900	Move (not copy) prevents the assay slot from accumulating stale credential bytes after promotion.
901	
902	[[rbsk_pinning_boundary]]
903	//axl_voices axk_premise
904	{rbsk_pinning_boundary}::
905	A build that consumes a sealed reliquary MUST carry zero unpinned aspects: every tool image and base image it pulls resolves, content-pinned, from project-controlled {gar_service} (the reliquary cohort and the enshrined anchors), never from a mutable upstream tag.
906	Reliquary _generation_ — the inscribe/conclave ceremony that mints the sealed cohort — is the one and only phase permitted to pull unpinned upstream images, since the first tool must be bootstrapped from somewhere.
907	+
908	The boundary is staged on the reliquary itself: once a reliquary is sealed, everything downstream of it is pinned; everything that produces it may not yet be.
909	The capture family obeys the same boundary as the made side — the capture image tool (`gcrane`) is a reliquary cohort member, so a sealed-reliquary capture (bole/wsl/podvm) resolves a pinned `gcrane`; only conclave, which builds the reliquary and therefore cannot resolve from it, rides the floating Google-hosted bootstrap builder.
910	+
911	Rationale: a result that depends on whatever tag happened to be current at build time is a versioning failure, not a convenience — the same anti-ambient-state ethos as content-addressed anchors, the depot tripwire, and SLSA vouch.
912	The narrow bootstrap exception is bounded, supervised, and re-runnable; it touches nothing a consumer relies on.
913	The durable supply-chain canon, the gcr.io-bootstrap-only provenance, and the per-grade trust details live in RBSCB.
914	
915	== Dependency Inventory
916	
917	{at_recipe_bottle} depends on a minimal, explicitly managed set of external commands.
918	The Bash Console Guide (`BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) establishes the universal dependency discipline — POSIX builtins preferred, external commands justified individually.
919	This section inventories the concrete choices {at_recipe_bottle} has made within that framework.
920	
921	=== Consumer Dependencies
922	
923	Required on every {at_workstation}.
924	Enforced by qualification and documented on the project page.
925	
926	[cols="1,3"]
927	|===
928	| Command | Purpose
929	
930	| `bash`
931	| Shell runtime (3.2+ compatible)
932	
933	| `git`
934	| Version control and subtree integration
935	
936	| `curl`
937	| HTTP client for all GCP REST API interaction
938	
939	| `jq`
940	| JSON parsing for API responses and configuration
941	
942	| `docker`
943	| {at_runtime} for bottle lifecycle
944	
945	| `openssl`
946	| JWT signing (RS256), base64 encoding/decoding, SHA-256 hashing
947	
948	| `ssh` / `scp` / `ssh-keygen`
949	| Secure credential transport and key generation
950

// Begin Mapping Section

// Category declarations:

// rbtgi_ prefix: RB Term Google Instance (Recipe Bottle specific instances)

// giam_ prefix: Google Cloud Identity and Access Management
// rbtc_ prefix: RB Term Colophon (tabtarget entry points)

// Local Architecture Terms (bottle deployment architecture):

// st_ prefix: Support Term (infrastructure and technical concepts)

// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//   _ed: past tense (verbs)
//   _ing: progressive form (verbs)
