// ============================================================================
// TIER 1: WHY AND WHAT
// ============================================================================

== System Overview

=== Core Trust Challenge

Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.

This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:

*
{st_container_s}
provide powerful tool isolation
* But they need controlled access to do useful work
* Standard
{st_container}
networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.

=== The Bottle Pattern

{at_rbm_system}
solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
{st_container}
type here referred to as a
{at_bottle_container}:

. File Access Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
sees only explicitly controlled set of
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No other access to
{at_workstation}
filesystem

. Network Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
has no direct network access
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container},
a
{st_container}
configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
* Only declared
{st_port_map_s}
and destinations allowed
* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic

Each
{rbsc_crucible}
maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:

* One
{at_sentry_container}
enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
* One
{at_pentacle_container}
establishing and configuring the network namespace
* One
{at_bottle_container}
running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
* Clear configuration in a single
{at_nameplate}
file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts

Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
{at_sentry_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_pentacle_container}
starts, and the
{at_pentacle_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_bottle_container}
starts.
This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
{at_recipe_bottle}
Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).

The
{at_pentacle_container}
is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
{at_sentry_container},
then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
{at_bottle_container}
via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
{at_bottle_container}
experiences only a functional path to its
{at_sentry_container}
gateway

=== Security Properties

The
{at_rbm_system}
maintains following essential guarantees:

. File Access Control
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
can access only explicitly configured
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No access to broader system resources

. Network Isolation
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container}
* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
* Only explicitly configured
{st_port_map_s}
allow traffic between
{at_transit_network}
and
{at_enclave_network}
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
{at_sentry_container_s}
* Comprehensive traffic logging

. Service Separation
* Each
{rbsc_crucible}
fully isolated
* No cross-service communication
* Independent security policies
* Clean state separation

. Service Access Control
* External access to services only permitted when
{rbrn_entry_mode}
is enabled
* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
* All service access must originate from outside the pod
* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
{at_sentry_container}
->
{at_bottle_container}

These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
{at_bottle_container}
(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.

=== Adversarial Validation: The Ifrit

include::RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc[]

== Getting Started

For builders and operators new to
{at_recipe_bottle},
this section provides conceptual grounding and the setup journey.

include::RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc[leveloffset=+2]

// ============================================================================
// TIER 2: HOW
// ============================================================================

== Google API Engagement

{at_recipe_bottle}
invokes
{gcp_service}
APIs through
{giam_service_account}
JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.

* *Authentication*:
{giam_service_account_s}
use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
These JWTs are exchanged for
{oauth_token_s}
via Google's token endpoint.
All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.

* *Invocation Pattern*:
Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.

* *Long-Running Operations*:
{gcp_service}
{gcp_lro_s}
return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
The system handles both success and error states.
Operations utilize
{rbbc_submit}
and
{rbbc_await}
to handle all asynchronous operations.

* *IAM Policies*:
All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
{gcp_service}
resources.

* *IAM Member Format*:
All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
+
GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
+
Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
+
Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.

== Operational Invariants

=== Concurrent Session Safety

{at_recipe_bottle}
commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.

Concurrency safety is achieved through:

* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.

* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
{rbtgi_repository}.

* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.

* *Semantic Preconditions*:
Operations like
{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
require clean git state.
This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.

NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.

NOTE: The
{gcb_service}
private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
Machine type is set at pool creation time via
{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
The
{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
See
{rbtgo_quota_build}
for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.

== Key Premises

[[rbsk_no_gcloud]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_no_gcloud}::
{at_recipe_bottle}
scripts MUST NOT invoke the `gcloud` CLI in local bash scripts or transitively through helper scripts.
All local
{gcp_service}
interaction uses direct REST API calls via `curl` with JWT-authenticated bearer tokens.
+
**Exception — vouch provenance fetch**: The {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verification step (conjure mode only) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` for SLSA provenance envelope retrieval.
This wraps complex Container Analysis API interaction that would be burdensome to replicate via raw REST.
All other GCB step operations use the GCE metadata server for OAuth token acquisition and direct registry REST API calls — no gcloud.
The provenance fetch runs as Python 3 on the gcloud builder image after the primary image is built; the reproducibility concern that motivates this premise is weaker for post-build verification.
Works under NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
+
In GCB build step contexts (conjure, bind, graft, enshrine), registry authentication uses the GCE metadata server to obtain an access token and writes a static Docker config JSON.
+
Rationale: `gcloud` is an opaque, version-sensitive binary that obscures API semantics, introduces implicit authentication state, and breaks reproducibility.
The `gcloud auth configure-docker` approach also fails mechanically in GCB steps — the gcloud builder image lacks `docker` in PATH, so the credential helper it installs cannot function.
+
NOTE: The gcloud reliquary image exists because every ordain-path build step (conjure, bind, graft) uses it for the about/vouch pipeline (`bash`, `date`, and `gcloud` for provenance retrieval per the exception above).
The reliquary toolchain is mode-uniform: all ordain modes consume gcloud, docker, alpine, and syft from the reliquary; bind additionally uses skopeo for image-copy.

[[rbsk_in_pool_preflight]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_in_pool_preflight}::
Every ordain-path Cloud Build job — conjure's combined image+about, bind's combined mirror+about, graft's combined about+vouch, and enshrine — prepends a step 0 (`rbgjr01-reliquary-preflight.sh`) that HEAD-checks reliquary tool images from the worker pool's vantage before expensive work runs.
Conjure additionally HEAD-checks the vessel's enshrined base anchors (slots 1-3 from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR`); other modes pass empty locator substitutions.
+
Coverage is 5 of 6 reliquary tools — gcloud, docker, syft, binfmt, skopeo.
Alpine, the preflight step's own image, is validated implicitly by Cloud Build pulling it before the script runs.
Failure produces a structured "this pool can't see «TOOL»" message naming each missed item, with remediation directive (re-inscribe + re-yoke + re-ordain).
+
Rationale: defense-in-depth on top of the host-side `zrbfd_registry_preflight` (which validates the Director's host-side view of GAR).
The in-pool check catches divergence the host-side check cannot — airgap private-VPC routing, Director↔Mason IAM split, and submit↔execute time skew.
+
Scope boundary: standalone vouch (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`) and standalone about (`zrbfv_about_submit`) are operator-recovery paths and are not currently covered.

[[rbsk_invest_serialized]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_invest_serialized}::
{rbtgo_retriever_invest}, {rbtgo_director_invest}, and {rbtgo_governor_mantle} write credential bytes to a singleton assay slot (`RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE`), not a per-identity slot.
The operator MUST move `RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE` to a safe place before initiating the next operation that writes to the assay slot — typically the role's production slot (`RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE`, `RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE`, or `RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE`) when the invester and the consumer are the same operator, otherwise a delivery channel to the consumer.
Two consecutive assay-slot writers without an intervening move lose the first writer's bytes.
Batched invest is not supported — by design.
+
Rationale: a singleton assay slot keeps the producer-output discipline uniform across roles and frees the implementation from path-space per-identity routing.
The operator-driven move step is the deliberate promotion gate from producer output to role-production consumer; it also acts as the operator's explicit acknowledgement of which identity owns the role at runtime, since `RBRA` files encode exactly one identity at a time.
Move (not copy) prevents the assay slot from accumulating stale credential bytes after promotion.

[[rbsk_pinning_boundary]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{rbsk_pinning_boundary}::
A build that consumes a sealed reliquary MUST carry zero unpinned aspects: every tool image and base image it pulls resolves, content-pinned, from project-controlled {gar_service} (the reliquary cohort and the enshrined anchors), never from a mutable upstream tag.
Reliquary _generation_ — the inscribe/conclave ceremony that mints the sealed cohort — is the one and only phase permitted to pull unpinned upstream images, since the first tool must be bootstrapped from somewhere.
+
The boundary is staged on the reliquary itself: once a reliquary is sealed, everything downstream of it is pinned; everything that produces it may not yet be.
The capture family obeys the same boundary as the made side — the capture image tool (`gcrane`) is a reliquary cohort member, so a sealed-reliquary capture (bole/wsl/podvm) resolves a pinned `gcrane`; only conclave, which builds the reliquary and therefore cannot resolve from it, rides the floating Google-hosted bootstrap builder.
+
Rationale: a result that depends on whatever tag happened to be current at build time is a versioning failure, not a convenience — the same anti-ambient-state ethos as content-addressed anchors, the depot tripwire, and SLSA vouch.
The narrow bootstrap exception is bounded, supervised, and re-runnable; it touches nothing a consumer relies on.
The durable supply-chain canon, the gcr.io-bootstrap-only provenance, and the per-grade trust details live in RBSCB.

== Dependency Inventory

{at_recipe_bottle} depends on a minimal, explicitly managed set of external commands.
The Bash Console Guide (`BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) establishes the universal dependency discipline — POSIX builtins preferred, external commands justified individually.
This section inventories the concrete choices {at_recipe_bottle} has made within that framework.

=== Consumer Dependencies

Required on every {at_workstation}.
Enforced by qualification and documented on the project page.

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Command | Purpose

| `bash`
| Shell runtime (3.2+ compatible)

| `git`
| Version control and subtree integration

| `curl`
| HTTP client for all GCP REST API interaction

| `jq`
| JSON parsing for API responses and configuration

| `docker`
| {at_runtime} for bottle lifecycle

| `openssl`
| JWT signing (RS256), base64 encoding/decoding, SHA-256 hashing

| `ssh` / `scp` / `ssh-keygen`
| Secure credential transport and key generation

file.content:
=== Nameplate Regime (RBRN)

[[rbrn_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced axrd_manifold
{rbrn_regime}::
Configuration regime for crucible deployment.
File-sourced, one per nameplate: an rbrn.env in each nameplate subdirectory under {rbsld_moorings}, the subdirectory named by the nameplate's {rbrn_moniker}.
Defines container deployment and network security settings.

[[rbrn_moniker]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_xname
{rbrn_moniker}::
Unique moniker for the crucible instance; 2-12 characters.

[[rbrn_description]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional rbst_description
{rbrn_description}::
Human-readable description of the service purpose; 0-120 characters.

[[rbrn_runtime]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axt_enumeration
{rbrn_runtime}::
Container runtime for service deployment.

[[rbrn_runtime_docker]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_runtime_docker}::
Use Docker container runtime.
Serialized as `docker` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_runtime_podman]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_runtime_podman}::
Use Podman container runtime.
Serialized as `podman` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_sentry_vessel]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_xname
{rbrn_sentry_vessel}::
Vessel identifier for the sentry container image.

[[rbrn_sentry_hallmark]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_hallmark
{rbrn_sentry_hallmark}::
Hallmark timestamp for the sentry container image.

[[rbrn_bottle_vessel]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_xname
{rbrn_bottle_vessel}::
Vessel identifier for the bottle container image.

[[rbrn_bottle_hallmark]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_hallmark
{rbrn_bottle_hallmark}::
Hallmark timestamp for the bottle container image.

[[rbrn_entry_mode]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axt_enumeration
{rbrn_entry_mode}::
Mode for user-initiated entry functionality.

[[rbrn_entry_mode_disabled]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_entry_mode_disabled}::
Entry functionality is disabled; no user-initiated connections.
Serialized as `rbnne_disabled` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_entry_mode_enabled]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_entry_mode_enabled}::
Entry functionality is enabled; user-initiated connections accepted.
Serialized as `rbnne_enabled` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_entry_port_workstation]]
//axvr_variable axd_conditional rbst_port
{rbrn_entry_port_workstation}::
External port exposed on the transit network for user entry.

[[rbrn_entry_port_enclave]]
//axvr_variable axd_conditional rbst_port
{rbrn_entry_port_enclave}::
Port used between sentry and bottle containers for entry traffic.

[[rbrn_enclave_base_ip]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_ipv4
{rbrn_enclave_base_ip}::
Base IPv4 address defining the enclave network range.

[[rbrn_enclave_netmask]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_netmask
{rbrn_enclave_netmask}::
Network mask width for enclave network addressing; 8-30.

[[rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_ipv4
{rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}::
IP address assigned to the sentry container within the enclave.

[[rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_ipv4
{rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}::
IP address assigned to the bottle container within the enclave.

[[rbrn_uplink_port_min]]
//axvr_variable axd_required rbst_port
{rbrn_uplink_port_min}::
Minimum port number for bottle-initiated outbound connections.

[[rbrn_uplink_dns_mode]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axt_enumeration
{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode}::
DNS resolution mode for bottle-initiated requests.

[[rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_disabled]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_disabled}::
DNS resolution disabled; no bottle-initiated DNS queries.
Serialized as `rbnne_disabled` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_global]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_global}::
DNS resolution globally enabled; all domains resolvable.
Serialized as `rbnne_global` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_allowlist]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode_allowlist}::
DNS resolution restricted to allowed domains.
Serialized as `rbnne_allowlist` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_access_mode]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axt_enumeration
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode}::
IP access mode for bottle-initiated connections.

[[rbrn_uplink_access_mode_disabled]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_disabled}::
IP access disabled; no bottle-initiated outbound connections.
Serialized as `rbnne_disabled` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_access_mode_global]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_global}::
IP access globally enabled; all destinations reachable.
Serialized as `rbnne_global` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}::
IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges.
Serialized as `rbnne_allowlist` in bash regime variable value.

[[rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs]]
//axvr_variable axd_conditional rbst_cidr_list
{rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs}::
CIDR ranges for allowed bottle-initiated outbound traffic.

[[rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains]]
//axvr_variable axd_conditional rbst_domain_list
{rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}::
Domain names allowed for bottle-initiated DNS resolution.

// Volume mount specifications relocated to per-nameplate compose fragments («moniker».compose.yml).
// Previously: RBRN_DOCKER_VOLUME_MOUNTS, RBRN_PODMAN_VOLUME_MOUNTS — semicolon-delimited strings
// passed as --volume args. Compose fragments use declarative volumes: sections instead.

[[rbrn_group_ark_reference]]
//axvr_group
{rbrn_group_ark_reference}::
Ark reference variables identifying which vessel hallmark to deploy.

[[rbrn_group_entry]]
//axvr_group axd_conditional
{rbrn_group_entry}::
Entry service variables for user-initiated connections.

[[rbrn_group_access_allowlist]]
//axvr_group axd_conditional
{rbrn_group_access_allowlist}::
Access allowlist variables for IP-restricted outbound traffic.

[[rbrn_group_dns_allowlist]]
//axvr_group axd_conditional
{rbrn_group_dns_allowlist}::
DNS allowlist variables for domain-restricted resolution.

content.content:
18:Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19:Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
45:Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
54:Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
77:_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
91:_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
145:  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
149:  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
151:_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
155:**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
160:VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
161:_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
162:_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
164:**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
167:Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
168:AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
170:_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
173:_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).
180:Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
181:_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
182:_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).
193:Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
198:An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
244:**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
246:A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
248:over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
249:Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
250:Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
251:_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
252:near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.

content.text:
Found and read everything. Here's where we stand.

**The memo is `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md`** (dated yesterday, 22KB). It's an intent/shaping document — nothing built — that supersedes the 2026-04-15 conduit memo and rides the ₣BZ citizen/capability model. Its load-bearing conclusions:

- **Scope is consume-only**: invoking native foundation models on Bedrock from a contained client. The host-your-own-weights path (embassy/bullion/Custom Model Import) collapses away. Bedrock is stateless and serverless — no instance to harden — which is exactly why Recipe Bottle holds the persistent config (§2).
- **Vocabulary settled**: **Quire** (the model endpoint, `endow`/`dissolve`, 0..N) and **Outpost** (the PrivateLink reach, `raise`/`strike`, 0..1, including both the AWS peer and the §11 local terminus container). No "submit" verb — workloads inside the bottle make the calls; RB's surface ends at "path and identity ready."
- **Six privacy boundaries**, with F (server-side agency egress) the subtle one — governed only by IAM, invisible to the sentry. The `agency` lattice (`none < internal < internet`) is its declared home and doubles as the future cross-Quire clearance lattice.
- **MVP**: single allowlist Quire, `agency: none`, confined credential, **no Outpost/VPC** — the memo is emphatic that this already delivers the privacy story; the VPC's one non-premature job is egress *exclusivity*.
- **Open items** (§10): prefix minting (`RBRQ` candidate; Outpost can't be `RBRO` — taken by OAuth), the **instance-vs-menu** question for what a Quire fundamentally is (menu/envelope is the lean), terminus consumer class (container-only is the lean), and the RBSHR cross-reference fix.

**RBS0 context, read against the memo**: The Quire config slots naturally into the `RBR*` regime family (eight siblings today: RBRR/RBRD/RBRP/RBRO/RBRV/RBRA/RBRN/RBRS, all rooted in `rbmm_moorings/`). The reach layer extends exactly the nameplate's existing uplink posture — `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE`/`DNS_MODE` with `disabled`/`global`/`allowlist` and the CIDR/domain allowlists — so the MVP Quire is close to "a nameplate whose allowlist is the Bedrock endpoint + STS and nothing else." The RBSHR roadmap still carries the predecessor framing: WireGuard-in-sentry (line ~155, which memo §11 explicitly rejects), the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" marked *Trigger: immediate*, and the chantry entry — all still breadcrumbed to the 2026-04-15 memo, not yesterday's.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AWS Bedrock cost controls and budget limits for 2026:

## Native Cost Control Limitations

There is no built-in way in AWS Bedrock to cap how many tokens a team can consume, or to automatically stop a runaway agent before it burns through your budget. Native Bedrock only provides visibility; for enforcement, a proxy pattern is unavoidable.

## Available Cost Control Options

AWS provides three main approaches to controlling Bedrock costs as of 2026:

1. **AWS Budgets with Cost Allocation Tags**: AWS Budgets can filter by tags and create a budget scoped to a specific team's Bedrock spend. When the spend hits 80%, 90%, or 100% of the budget, Budgets sends a notification via SNS or email. However, this is reactive rather than preventive.

2. **CloudWatch Alarms with Lambda**: This approach uses Bedrock's built-in invocation logging to feed CloudWatch metrics, then reacts to threshold breaches with an alarm that triggers a Lambda.

3. **Application-Layer Gateway**: The limit is checked synchronously before every call for real-time enforcement, though this adds latency overhead.

## Per-Model Spending Caps

AWS Bedrock supports setting individual budgets for different Amazon Bedrock models based on their pricing and usage patterns, with a default budget fallback if no specific budget is defined for a model. This capability is available through the Generative AI Gateway Solution, which uses LiteLLM.

## Recommended Starting Point

Start with AWS Budgets + Tags if you need visibility without writing code. Move to CloudWatch + Lambda if you need automated blocking at the team level. Use the App-Layer Gateway only if you need per-user real-time enforcement or are building a multi-tenant product.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Bedrock application inference profiles, cost allocation tags, and per-model budget tracking:

## Application Inference Profiles Overview

Application inference profiles (AIPs) let you attribute Amazon Bedrock costs by application, team, or workload for the Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel and Converse APIs on the bedrock-runtime endpoint. An application inference profile is a resource that references a specific Amazon Bedrock model. You create the profile, attach cost allocation tags, and then use the profile ARN in place of the model ID in your API calls.

## Cost Allocation Tags and Per-Model Tracking

Each AIP is model-specific and carries cost allocation tags that flow to AWS Cost Explorer and AWS Cost and Usage Reports (CUR 2.0). You can create custom inference profiles for base FMs and apply cost allocation tags like department, team, and application identifiers. These tags integrate with AWS cost management tools including AWS Cost Explorer, AWS Budgets, and AWS Cost Anomaly Detection, enabling detailed cost analysis and budget control.

## Key Constraints

Each application inference profile is tied to a specific model. This means you need a separate profile for every unique combination of model, team, and tag set.

Tags can take up to 24 hours to appear in Cost Explorer and CUR after activation. Cost allocation tags are not retroactive. Only costs incurred after activation are tagged.

## Cost Analysis Tools

After tag activation, you can analyze Amazon Bedrock costs by application inference profile in the following tools: AWS Cost Explorer – Filter by profile tags to view cost trends over time. Group by tag to compare costs across profiles. AWS Cost and Usage Reports (CUR 2.0) – Query CUR data for line-item cost breakdowns by profile tag.

## Budget Management

Organizations can use services like AWS Budgets to set tag-based budgets and alarms to monitor usage, and receive alerts for anomalies or predefined thresholds. This scalable, programmatic approach eliminates inefficient manual processes, reduces the risk of excess spending, and ensures that critical applications receive priority.

I've completed a web search for your query on AWS Budgets actions, IAM policy denial, and Bedrock cost management. Here are the key findings:

## AWS Budgets Actions with IAM Policies

Your available actions include applying an IAM policy or a service control policy (SCP). Budget Actions can automatically apply IAM policies that prevent new resource creation, or apply Service Control Policies that restrict an entire account.

## Bedrock-Specific Cost Control

For Amazon Bedrock specifically, there is no built-in way to cap how many tokens a team can consume, or to automatically stop a runaway agent before it burns through your budget. However, there are ways to implement hard caps:

The most effective option is updating an IAM deny policy attached to the team's role. You tag your IAM roles with keys like team or cost-center. AWS Budgets can filter by those tags and create a budget scoped to a specific team's Bedrock spend. 

When the spend hits 80%, 90%, or 100% of the budget, Budgets sends a notification via SNS or email. You can also attach a Lambda action to the budget alert for automated responses — for example, posting to Slack, opening a ticket, or triggering the same IAM policy update.

Once the policy is updated, any new session from that team is blocked — regardless of what allow policies are attached to the role.

This approach effectively creates a hard cap by using deny policies that are automatically triggered when budget thresholds are reached.

Based on the search results, here's information about Amazon Bedrock service quotas for tokens per minute and requests per minute:

## Service Quota Types

Model inference in Amazon Bedrock is controlled by quotas on token usage. Tokens Per Minute (TPM) controls the total token throughput across your account, while Requests Per Minute (RPM) limits the number of API calls you can make. However, Amazon Bedrock no longer enforces requests-per-minute (RPM) quotas on the bedrock-runtime endpoint.

## Per-Model Quotas

All major Bedrock models have TPM quotas ranging from 200K to 8M TPM depending on the model, with newer models like Claude Sonnet 4.6 offering 5M TPM. Amazon Bedrock offers two inference endpoints – bedrock-runtime and bedrock-mantle – each with its own per-model quota allocations.

## Adjustable Quotas

If a quota is marked as Yes, you can adjust it by following the steps at Requesting a Quota Increase in the Service Quotas User Guide. These quotas are adjustable through AWS Service Quotas, allowing you to request increases as your application scales.

## Throttling

The request is throttled if you exceed a quota. When requests are throttled due to quota limits, you'll receive a ThrottlingException and should implement retry logic with exponential backoff.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about restricting Bedrock InvokeModel to specific model ARNs using IAM policies with deny conditions:

## Deny Policies for Specific Model ARNs

To prevent a user from invoking foundation models, you need to deny access to API actions that invoke models directly. The following example shows an identity-based policy that denies access to running inference on a specific model.

### Example: Deny All Except Approved Models

An IAM policy example using NotResource that denies the models except Amazon Nova models uses a deny statement for bedrock:InvokeModel and bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream actions with NotResource specifying the approved model ARNs.

### Resource ARN Formats

For Bedrock model restrictions, you can target specific resources:
- Foundation model ARN format: `arn:aws:bedrock:*::foundation-model/amazon.nova-lite-v1:0`
- Inference profile ARN format: `arn:aws:bedrock:*::inference-profile/us.amazon.nova-lite-v1:0`

### Wildcard Pattern Approach

By using an asterisk (*) for the model ID in your policies, you can enable access to a broader set of foundation models by default and then create separate deny policies for select models that aren't approved in your organization.

### Condition Key Usage

You can add a condition to your policies to limit access to actions and resources. For example, you can write a policy condition to specify that all requests must be sent using SSL. Additionally, you can use IAM policies to permit or deny Invoke* actions for specific foundation models within individual AWS accounts.

### Inference Profile ARN Condition

For more specific control with inference profiles, you can use a Condition with bedrock:InferenceProfileArn to restrict access based on the inference profile ARN.

To prevent a user from invoking foundation models, you need to deny access to API actions that invoke models directly. The following example shows an identity-based policy that denies access to running inference on a specific model.

### Example: Deny All Except Approved Models

An IAM policy example using NotResource that denies the models except Amazon Nova models uses a deny statement for bedrock:InvokeModel and bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream actions with NotResource specifying the approved model ARNs.

### Resource ARN Formats

For Bedrock model restrictions, you can target specific resources:
- Foundation model ARN format: `arn:aws:bedrock:*::foundation-model/amazon.nova-lite-v1:0`
- Inference profile ARN format: `arn:aws:bedrock:*::inference-profile/us.amazon.nova-lite-v1:0`

### Wildcard Pattern Approach

By using an asterisk (*) for the model ID in your policies, you can enable access to a broader set of foundation models by default and then create separate deny policies for select models that aren't approved in your organization.

### Condition Key Usage

You can add a condition to your policies to limit access to actions and resources. For example, you can write a policy condition to specify that all requests must be sent using SSL. Additionally, you can use IAM policies to permit or deny Invoke* actions for specific foundation models within individual AWS accounts.

### Inference Profile ARN Condition

For more specific control with inference profiles, you can use a Condition with bedrock:InferenceProfileArn to restrict access based on the inference profile ARN.

content.text:
Searches done — here's the picture, and it maps onto the Quire shaping surprisingly cleanly.

## The headline

**Constraining available models: airtight, native, free.** IAM scopes `bedrock:InvokeModel` to an explicit list of model or inference-profile ARNs — allowlist or deny-all-but. The Quire's "governed invocation principal" from memo §9 is *exactly* this object; the menu framing's "model is a list of approved options" is directly enforceable today with zero standing infrastructure.

**Constraining daily expenditure: strong attribution, but no native hard cap.** AWS is explicit about this — Bedrock has no built-in "stop at $X" switch. What exists is a ladder of mechanisms, ordered by reaction time:

| Rung | Mechanism | Grain | Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Application inference profiles** + cost-allocation tags | per-call attribution | One AIP per (model, purpose); invoke via profile ARN; costs flow to Cost Explorer / CUR / Budgets per tag. The *accounting* primitive. |
| 2 | **AWS Budgets + budget actions** | ~hours-to-a-day | Threshold breach can *automatically attach an IAM deny policy* — a real cutoff, but billing data lags, so a runaway day can overshoot before it trips. |
| 3 | **CloudWatch token metrics + alarm + Lambda deny** | ~minutes | Bedrock emits token-count metrics (dimensionable per inference profile) *without* invocation logging — privacy-compatible metering. An alarm-triggered Lambda attaches the deny. |
| 4 | **Counting proxy in the data path** (LiteLLM-style gateway) | real-time, pre-call | The only true synchronous cap. AWS's own guidance concedes this is the unavoidable answer for hard enforcement. |

(TPM service quotas exist per model per account and bound burn *rate* — a structural worst-case ceiling — but they're account-grain and only adjustable upward self-serve, so they're a backstop, not a knob.)

## Thoughts on the musing

Your service idea is well-shaped, and the research sharpens it into one pleasing confirmation and one genuine fork.

**The confirmation:** rungs 1–3 compose into something very Recipe-Bottle-shaped without violating §4's "no submit verb." `endow` would mint a *bundle*: the IAM principal scoped to the Quire's model list, the AIP(s) with the Quire's tags, the budget with a declared daily allowance, and the cutoff wiring. RB never touches the inference path — it establishes and maintains the binding, and the binding now includes the cost envelope. This is the same intent-vs-enforcement spine as the `agency` field: declared allowance, IAM-enforced cutoff, auditable diff between the two. It also feeds the menu framing — "cost is an allowance/cap" stops being aspirational prose and becomes three concrete AWS resources per Quire.

**The fork:** how hard is "daily expenditure constraint" allowed to be?

- **Soft cap (rungs 2–3):** cutoff lands minutes-to-hours after breach; worst case you overshoot by some bounded slop. Zero data-path presence, fits the memo as written. For a solo/small private deployment where the threat is *your own runaway agent*, minutes-grain is honestly fine — the overshoot is dollars, not hundreds.
- **Hard cap (rung 4):** requires a metering proxy *in the inference path* — which is a posture change the memo deliberately avoided. The interesting wrinkle: the §11 terminus is already a container sitting on the reach path in the fast-follow. A terminus that grows a token meter is architecturally coherent... but it would have to terminate TLS to count tokens, which spends privacy boundary A to enforce a cost boundary. I'd name that trade explicitly rather than back into it.

My lean: the MVP Quire declares `allowance` and gets rungs 1+2 (attribution + day-grain auto-deny); rung 3 is the fast-follow sweetener (minutes-grain, still privacy-clean); rung 4 is a named non-goal unless a multi-user deployment makes overshoot-slop real money. That keeps "Recipe Bottle governs the envelope, the consumer picks within it" intact — the envelope just gains teeth.

One more observation: the deny-policy cutoff mechanism is *per principal*, and the principal is the Quire's identity. So in the future multi-Quire choreography, each Quire's allowance trips independently — the cost lattice gets the same per-node enforcement the agency lattice has. The shape keeps rhyming, which I take as another "the shape is right" signal like the one in §9.

Worth a short addendum to the memo (§6 cost-envelope fields + this enforcement ladder), or shall we keep mulling first?

Sources:
- [How to limit usage and cost on Amazon Bedrock — AWS Builder Center](https://builder.aws.com/content/3DIYzXtsMwvVh1jsn1CSpa8k2rL/limiting-bedrock-usage-and-controlling-costs)
- [Application inference profiles — Amazon Bedrock docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/cost-mgmt-application-inference-profiles.html)
- [Track, allocate, and manage your generative AI cost and usage with Amazon Bedrock — AWS blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/track-allocate-and-manage-your-generative-ai-cost-and-usage-with-amazon-bedrock/)
- [Configuring budget actions — AWS Cost Management docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/budgets-controls.html)
- [Build a proactive AI cost management system for Amazon Bedrock — AWS blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/build-a-proactive-ai-cost-management-system-for-amazon-bedrock-part-1/)
- [Quotas for Amazon Bedrock — docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/quotas.html)
- [Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Bedrock — docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)
- [How to Track and Limit Amazon Bedrock Usage by User — AWS re:Post](https://repost.aws/articles/ARoDnASCxDQyGFfaagReMZNw/how-to-track-and-limit-amazon-bedrock-usage-by-user)

message.content: Well I don't want to create a 'crap wrap', i.e. non-load-bearing software layer: needs to have good rationale.  An approach I want to noodle with you here is an analog to Governor/Director/Retriever for images: one role that administers others, then constrained users that have soft and hard caps, and possibly limits on what models they use and what internet access the models get.  Thoughts?  This might start to go beyond what bash can reasonably do and instead be a program running in a Sentry/Outpost analog

content.text:
This analog holds up well under the crap-wrap test — let me say why, and then push on the "beyond bash" intuition, because I think the strain shows up somewhere other than where you're pointing.

## Why this layer is load-bearing

The test from the GCP trio is instructive: Governor/Director/Retriever isn't a wrapper over GCP IAM — IAM doesn't *have* roster, invest, divest, singleton-per-role, or the assay-slot promotion gate. The layer codifies a **lifecycle ceremony** AWS-style primitives don't compose on their own. The Bedrock version passes the same test, and the evidence is last turn's ladder: a "constrained user" is an IAM principal + a per-model AIP set + activated cost tags + a budget + a budget-action deny wiring + an agency-deny clause. AWS gives you each piece; **nothing native composes them into one named, auditable object.** Hand-wiring that per user is exactly the gap-between-intent-and-behavior the layer removes. Two concrete teeth:

- **Cost tags are non-retroactive.** A mis-established binding silently loses attribution data forever. Ceremony that guarantees establishment order (AIP → tag activation → budget → action) is load-bearing in the strictest sense.
- **Drift audit.** "Declared allowance/model-list/agency vs. actual IAM+Budgets state" is the same intent-vs-enforcement diff ₣BZ builds for GCP. Without the layer there's no declared side to diff against.

So: not crap wrap. It's the same move RB already made once, pointed at a second cloud.

## The mapping, with one structural difference

| GCP trio | Bedrock analog |
|---|---|
| Governor (invests/divests, rosters) | Admin principal — mints/retires constrained users, owns the envelope machinery |
| Director / Retriever | **N constrained citizens**, each: model allowlist (IAM), agency ceiling (IAM deny on agentic surfaces), soft cap (budget alert), hard cap (budget-action / alarm-Lambda deny) |

The difference worth noticing: the GCP trio divides *function* — three fixed roles with different jobs. Your Bedrock users divide *people/workloads* — N instances of the same shape with different ceilings. That's less a trio and more **Governor + citizens**, which is precisely ₣BZ's citizen/capability frame; the memo already says a Quire carries "a capability reference (who may invoke)." This musing is arguably just the AWS instantiation of the citizen model — a good sign, not a new invention.

And the "internet access of the models" limit costs nothing extra: that's the `agency` lattice, enforced as IAM denies on the agentic API surfaces (Bedrock Agents, AgentCore). Per-citizen agency ceilings fall out of the same principal.

## Where bash actually runs out — a twist

Here's the part I want to push back on gently: **almost none of the above needs a resident program.** Invest/divest/roster/endow are control-plane REST calls — establishment ceremony, bash's home turf. The enforcement *reactors* are AWS-resident and event-driven: Budgets actions and CloudWatch-alarm Lambdas run on AWS's clock, not in any process you keep alive. A shipped Lambda is a template artifact (the memo's "stood up from Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates"), not a daemon RB operates. Soft caps, hard-at-minutes caps, model lists, agency ceilings — all of it works with **zero standing RB software**, preserving the memo's "no instance to secure" win.

The genuine bash strain is somewhere else entirely: **SigV4.** Every AWS REST call needs the SigV4 request-signing dance — canonical request, multiple HMAC derivation steps, per-service/per-region scoping. It's mechanically doable in bash+openssl, but it's the kind of cryptographic ceremony where the GCP side's JWT-exchange was *one* signing operation and SigV4 is a signature *per request*. That's the honest "beyond what bash can reasonably do" line — it shows up at the transport layer on day one, not at the caps. If a program enters the MVP, I'd expect it to be a small **signing/REST helper** (theurge-adjacent Rust is right there), not a resident gateway.

The Sentry/Outpost-analog resident program is real but belongs to exactly one job: the **rung-4 real-time meter**, if minutes-grain hardness ever stops being enough. And when that day comes, the §11 terminus is its natural body — a trusted local container already positioned on the reach path, growing a meter. I'd keep it a named non-goal until a multi-user deployment makes overshoot-slop real money, because it spends both "no standing software" and (via TLS origination) complicates boundary A.

## One hazard to name

Don't let the word "hard cap" overclaim. AWS-native hardness is *minutes-grain* hardness — a cutoff that lands after bounded slop, not a synchronous wall. For the spec this wants honest vocabulary: maybe the citizen's envelope declares `soft` (notify) and `firm` (auto-deny, bounded overshoot), reserving `hard` for the proxy tier that doesn't exist yet. That keeps the lattice honest the same way `agency: none` keeps privacy honest — the guarantee is an explicit, named property, not a hopeful adjective.

Where my head lands: the role analog is sound and load-bearing, the citizen model is its frame, and the staged build is — establishment ceremony (bash or bash+signing-helper), AWS-native enforcement, terminus-meter deferred. Want me to capture this as a §-addendum to the quire-shaping memo while it's warm, or keep noodling?

message.content: Well lets get some new 'cult nouns' settled.  By the way: I started calling the rigorous naming pattern 'cult verbs' and 'cult nouns' because Opus once teased/ complained about that to me.  Is there an industry referent to that somehow?  I think we need catchy but distinct terms for these.  Can you seek out Envoy in this repo?  I think we started a batch of proto-AWS names once upon a time.

attachment.snippet:
1	## Job Jockey Configuration
2	
3	Job Jockey (JJ) is installed for managing project initiatives.
4	
5	**Concepts:**
6	- **Heat**: Bounded initiative with coherent goals that are clear and present (3-50 officia). Status: `racing` (active execution) or `stabled` (paused for planning). Location: `current/` or `retired/` (done).
7	- **Pace**: Discrete action within a heat.
8	- **Itch**: Future work (any detail level), lives in jji_itch.md
9	- **Scar**: Closed work with lessons learned, lives in jjs_scar.md
10	- **Spook**: Team infrastructure stumble — any workflow failure improvable with deft attention. Capture as a pace when encountered, don't lose the current thread.
11	- **Cinch**: A decision settled in a paddock or docket and not to be re-litigated — both noun and verb ("cinch the approach," "the inscribe-skip is cinched"). Distinct from a *lock* in the concurrency sense (JJK's git-ref commit lock), which keeps that word.
12	
13	**Sincerity over efficiency:** When you notice something — a pattern, a concern, an insight about the work or the collaboration itself — say it. Discovery through conversation is part of the work, not a detour from it.
14	
15	**Docket posture:** A docket is a specification of needed change, not architectural commentary. It articulates what done looks like — the completion criterion goes under a `## Done when` heading (never a bare `## Done`, which misreads as a record of work already finished) — and any cinched constraints, which go under a `## Cinched` heading (never `## Locked`: *lock* is reserved for the concurrency-sense git-ref commit lock); it points at sources rather than restating them. A short `## Character` line naming the cognitive posture (e.g., "intricate but mechanical," "design conversation requiring judgment") earns its keep; the rest of the docket should resist filling in.
16	
17	**Slate-time vs mount-time.** The slate agent has just done analysis; the mount agent will reorient against the project as it stands then, with CLAUDE.md and specs already loaded. Hand off goal and boundary, not the analysis. Depth belongs in the slate commit message, not the docket body.
18	
19	**Stale-by-mount filter.** Each line must still be true at mount-time. Fail (drift): line numbers, byte/match counts, version strings, exhaustive site enumerations. Pass (durable): file paths as entry points, commit hashes, spec/concept names.
20	
21	**Docket anti-patterns:**
22	- Restating commits/specs the mount agent already loads. Point, don't paraphrase.
23	- Pre-baking implementation (function names, signatures, locations for new code). Mount-time decisions.
24	- Site enumerations. Write a discovery recipe (`grep "pattern" Tools/rbk/`) instead of listing six file:line entries.
25	- Body over ~15 lines warrants suspicion of overprescription.
26	- **Plan-step structure stays in the plan.** Docket phase/step labels (A/B/C, "Phase 1", "first/then/finally") must not appear as code comments — the plan won't exist at maintenance time, and line order already conveys execution.
27	- **Absolute paths to working trees.** Don't pin a docket to a specific repo clone (`/Users/foo/projects/your-project`, `~/proj/`). Mount may legitimately run against a different clone — if work needs to happen in *the* working directory, say so without naming it. Cross-repo coupling, if real, belongs in the paddock as a heat-shape note, not pace prose. Absolute paths *are* acceptable for external data roots (log dirs, `/tmp/...` scratch, machine-pinned data) — but flag those as operator-mutable rather than identity.
28	
29	**Reference discipline.** Pace order is the dependency tree — single-operator workflow runs paces in heat order, so explicit dependency markers in docket prose are usually overspecified. Coronet cross-refs in dockets earn their keep only when the dependency crosses heats or skips order — rare, not never.
30	
31	**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, cinched decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.
32	
33	**Silks never appear in artifact prose.** Paddock and docket *prose* must never name heat silks — neither a heat's own silks nor another heat's. Silks are display names that evolve via `jjx_relabel`; the moment one drifts, the prose that quoted it is stale and quietly misleads. This is the same failure mode as the coronet-in-paddock rule above, applied to the third identity type, and it completes a three-way distinction by how each kind fares against the lifecycle:
34	- **Firemarks** — lifecycle-bound (a heat keeps its firemark for life) → fine in both paddock and docket prose.
35	- **Coronets** — stable but invalidated by pace-state ops (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) → barred from paddock prose; in docket prose only for genuine cross-order or cross-heat dependencies (see **Reference discipline**).
36	- **Silks** — evolve freely via relabel → barred from *both* paddock and docket prose (this rule).
37	
38	Refer to a heat or pace by its purpose, not its display name; the firemark — or, where dockets allow, the coronet — is the durable handle. When editing a paddock or docket for other reasons, prune any silks-shaped kebab strings you find, as you would a stray coronet.
39	
40	**Semantic linefeeds in artifact prose.** Author paddock and docket prose — the bodies you write into `gazette_in.md` — with *semantic line breaks*: one sentence per line, and a long sentence broken at its major internal boundaries (em-dash, semicolon, colon, comma-before-conjunction). Markdown soft-wraps consecutive non-blank lines into one rendered paragraph, so this is invisible in the output and costs nothing; what it buys is the diff. Paddocks and dockets are edited incrementally and every edit is a commit, so one-thought-per-line makes a diff show the sentence that changed instead of re-flowing a 400-character paragraph as a single unreadable `+` line. Do not break at the word level — when you want word-grain diffs, that is `git diff --word-diff`, not physically split prose. (Instantiates JJS0's *Diff-Friendly Prose* principle, which governs gazette markdown only — not the `.adoc` specs, whose source answers to MCM line-break discipline.)
41	
42	**Mid-execution posture.** When a failure or surprise surfaces while a pace is mounted, the default response is mechanism + one specific repair you'd attempt. Do not proliferate options or weigh consequences across alternatives — pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. If the repair is obvious, proceed; if not, surface the question and stop.
43	
44	**Identities vs Display Names:**
45	- **Firemark**: Heat identity, two characters from a nonstandard b64 alphabet (e.g., `₣AA`, `₣Av`, `₣A-`, `₣A_`, `₣A7`). Used in command params and JSON keys.
46	- **Coronet**: Pace identity, firemark plus three characters from the same alphabet (e.g., `₢AAAAk`, `₢A-AAm`, `₢A_A-p`, `₢AAA_7`). Used in command params and JSON keys.
47	- **Silks**: kebab-case display name. Human-readable only — NOT usable for lookups.
48	
49	When a command takes a firemark or coronet, provide the identity, not the silks.
50	
51	**Display discipline (agent text output to user):**
52	- **Always show full coronets and firemarks; never abbreviate.** Write
53	  `₢A-AA-`, never `AA-`. Write `₣A-`, never `A-`. The operator works in
54	  several heats simultaneously; a bare 3-char pace suffix is ambiguous and
55	  forces re-derivation of the heat from context. The ₣/₢ glyph + heat half

185	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
186	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
187	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
188	
189	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
190	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
191	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
192	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
193	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
194	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
195	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
196	
197	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
198	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
199	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
200	{rbtgi_depot}
201	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
202	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
203	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
204	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
205	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
206	design conversation pending heat.
207	
208	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
209	A
210	{giam_service_account}
211	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
212	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
213	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
215	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
216	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
217	
218	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
219	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
220	A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
221	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
222	bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
223	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
224	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
225	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
226	Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.
227	
228	**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
229	Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
230	Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
231	Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
232	A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
233	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
234	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
235	
236	**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
237	Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
238	The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
239	Director holds no AWS credentials;
240	failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
241	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
242	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
243	
244	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
245	Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
246	A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
247	{at_bottle_container}
248	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
249	Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
250	Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
251	_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
252	near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
253	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
254	`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
255	Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
256	
257	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
258	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
259	Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.

The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.

**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.

**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).

{rbtgi_depot}
creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
A
{giam_service_account}
in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.

Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.

**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
Director holds no AWS credentials;
failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.

**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
{at_bottle_container}
over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.

`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;

input.description: Check candidate ecclesiastical names for repo collisions

content.text:
Candidates are all collision-clean. Three things to report, and the third is fun.

## The proto-AWS batch — found

It lives in **RBSHR, the "Cross-cloud architectural stance" cluster** (`RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:189-255`): **embassy** (AWS-side delivery premises), **envoy** (GCP-side federated cross-post actor, no resting AWS keys), **bullion** (content-addressed delivered artifact), **entrust** (director-triggered cross-post), **accredit/recall** (governor verbs for embassy binding), **chantry** (the Bedrock endpoint — Quire's ancestor). All flagged as "tentative working language; concepts are firmer than the words."

The load-bearing find isn't the names, it's the stance sentence right above them: *"If Recipe Bottle ever needs to build in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift."* Your Governor-analog musing from yesterday is exactly that pre-authorized decision arriving — consume-side rather than build-side, but the roadmap already reserved the slot. The hierarchy isn't drift; it was anticipated and parked. Note the register split the batch establishes: AWS-facing things speak **diplomatic/foreign-court** (embassy, envoy, accredit) because of asymmetric sovereignty, while the Quire memo's nouns went **ecclesiastical** (Quire, endow — heir to chantry).

## Industry referent for "cult nouns"

The mainstream referent is **Ubiquitous Language** from Eric Evans's Domain-Driven Design — a rigorously maintained vocabulary shared verbatim across code, docs, and conversation within a bounded context. That's the practice you're doing, at unusual intensity. Library science calls the artifact a **controlled vocabulary**. But the *linguistics* term for an in-group's distinctive working lexicon is **cant** (as in thieves' cant) or **argot**.

And here's the gift: *cant* derives from Latin **cantare, "to sing"** — the same root as *chant* and *chantry*. So the technically correct term for your cult nouns is a word that literally means singing, and your Bedrock register is already choral. The lexicon was apparently self-aware before we were. (Opus's tease also lands fairly — theurge, ifrit, pentacle, conclave *is* an occult shelf — but "cult nouns/verbs" communicates instantly and I'd keep it as the informal term, with "ubiquitous language" as the answer when an outsider asks if this is a known practice.)

## The new nouns — a proposal

Riding the Quire's ecclesiastical register, which turns out to have the exact offices waiting:

| Concept | Noun | Why it's load-bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Admin role — mints/retires constrained users, owns envelopes | **Precentor** | The cathedral officer who *directs the quire*. Administers who sings and what they sing — model lists and conduct, not just money. |
| Constrained user — capped principal invoking within bounds | **Chorister** | A member of the quire, granted a place, voicing only within the precentor's direction. The N-cardinality citizen shape from yesterday. |
| The per-chorister allowance envelope — caps, model list, agency ceiling | **Prebend** | The actual medieval term for a fixed allowance endowed to a cathedral office-holder. A chorister *holds a prebend*; spend-cap-as-endowed-allowance is the literal historical meaning. |

All three grep clean across Tools/ and Memos/, all keep faith with Quire/chantry/endow (a chantry was *funded by an endowment* — the family was always about governed allowances), and the diplomatic register stays free for the delivery-side batch (embassy/envoy), keeping the two AWS efforts distinguishable by ear.

Alternatives, one line each: **Almoner** (distributes funds — apt if the role is purely fiscal, but it isn't; it also governs models and agency); **Bursar** (treasurer — same narrowness, more familiar); **Cantor** (precentor's synonym — shorter, but collides conceptually with `cant` wordplay and sounds like "can't"). For verbs I'd resist minting: Governor's **invest/divest** carry over naturally (a precentor invests a chorister), though if you ever want register-true alternatives, canon law offers **induct** (into a benefice) and **deprive** (of one) — noted, not proposed.

One honest hesitation to flag: *prebend* is the most obscure word of the three — most readers will need the definition once. I think it earns that cost because nothing common says "endowed allowance with conditions attached," but if it grates, **stipend** is the plain cousin.

Want me to noodle these against the minting checklist (prefix space, terminal exclusivity) or let them breathe as concept nouns first?
